title: Shift register: many outputs, fewer pins
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire a 74HC595 shift register between three GPIOs and the LED bar, upload the Arduino sketch, and watch a single lit LED flow along the bar and back. The real lesson is the trade underneath it: Day 6 spent one pin per light, and today you spend a little time instead — feeding eight bits down a single wire — so three pins drive all eight lights. Learn how the data, clock, and latch wires split that job, and the same three pins will drive far bigger banks of outputs later.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the 74HC595 flowing light: the chip straddles the breadboard with GPIO 12 on its data pin, GPIO 13 on its latch pin, and GPIO 14 on its clock pin, and outputs Q0 to Q7 driving the LED bar graph through eight 220 ohm resistors, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: 74HC595 shift register
parts.explanation: The 74HC595 takes data one bit at a time on a single wire and presents it as eight parallel outputs. Three GPIO pins — data, clock, and latch — can drive eight LEDs, and chips can be chained for eight more. It's how a small board controls a big row of outputs.

parts.name: LED bar graph
parts.explanation: A LED bar packs ten separate LEDs into one strip, with a row of pins along each edge. Each LED is wired and driven on its own — exactly like the single LED from earlier days, just ten of them in a tidy line.

parts.name: 8 × 220 Ω resistors
parts.explanation: A resistor is a small component whose whole job is to limit how much current flows through a wire. Current is the flow of electricity through the circuit; a resistor throttles that flow down to a safe amount. Its strength is measured in ohms (Ω) — a larger number resists harder and lets less current through. A part like a LED wired straight to power pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out at once, so it sits in series with a resistor whose value is chosen to hold the current gentle enough to keep it alive. The coloured bands print the resistor's value, and it works the same either way round — a resistor has no positive or negative end.

parts.name: Jumper wires (about 15)
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

wiring.reason: Carries the byte into the chip one bit at a time.
One rising edge here presents all eight stored bits at once.
Each tick tells the chip to take in the next bit.
Eight chip outputs each drive one segment through its own resistor.
Held low so the outputs stay switched on.
Held high so the chip never clears what it holds.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical connection at a time — about fifteen jumpers is the most wiring so far — and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
The chip's half-moon notch sets its orientation; verify it matches the chart before checking anything else.
Teach the core trade — Day 6 spent one pin per LED in parallel; the 74HC595 spends time instead, taking eight bits one at a time so three pins drive eight outputs.

Give each wire a concrete job — data (GPIO 12) carries one bit at a time, each clock tick (GPIO 14) shifts the row and stores that bit, and the latch edge (GPIO 13) copies all eight to the outputs at once.
Connect it to the code — shiftOut sends the whole byte bit by bit, the latch reveals it, and x <<= 1 shifting the single 1 bit is exactly why the light walks the bar.
If nothing lights, check the chip's power first — VCC and MR (pin 10) to 5V, GND and OE (pin 13) to ground — before touching code.
If the pattern is scrambled, verify the three control wires against the sketch — data on GPIO 12, latch on GPIO 13, clock on GPIO 14.
If a single LED stays dark, check that segment's 220 Ω resistor and the bar's direction, which the learner met on Day 6.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up, and its library takes pins in the order data then latch then clock.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Straddle the 74HC595 across the breadboard's centre channel with its notch matching the chart.
Give the chip power — VCC and MR (pin 10) to 5V, GND and OE (pin 13) to ground.
Run the three control wires — GPIO 12 to DS (pin 14), GPIO 13 to ST_CP (pin 12), GPIO 14 to SH_CP (pin 11).
Connect outputs Q0–Q7 to the LED bar, with a 220 Ω resistor in series for each segment.
Check the LED bar's direction against the chart — you saw on Day 6 how easily it reverses.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_14.1_FlowingLight02.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Watch one lit LED run along the bar and back.

arduino.explanation: Sends the byte to the chip one bit at a time — a level on GPIO 12 for each bit, and a clock tick on GPIO 14 to take it in. One call does all eight.
The rising edge on GPIO 13 copies all eight stored bits to the outputs in one step, so the whole pattern appears together.
Slides the single 1 bit one place left, so the lit LED takes one step along the bar; delay(50) sets the pace.

micropython.explanation: The module takes its pins in the order data then latch then clock — matching today's wiring — with -1 meaning OE is simply wired to ground.
Sends the byte and latches it in one call; the first argument picks which direction the pattern travels.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: A shift register trades time for pins — it takes eight bits one at a time down a single wire, clocked in one per tick, then a single latch edge lights all eight outputs together.
theoryModel.formula: 8 bits down 1 data wire, one per clock tick → one latch edge → 8 outputs lit together

notes.title: What the latch buys you
notes.body: Without it, every LED would flicker as the bits shifted through on their way to their final slot. The latch holds the outputs steady until the whole byte has arrived, then updates them together — you only ever see finished patterns.

notes.title: Where Day 6's ten pins went, and beyond
notes.body: Day 6 gave every segment its own GPIO. Here the chip holds all eight states and the board keeps just three wires. The same three can drive far more — chips chain end to end, each passing bits to the next, so three pins can run sixteen, twenty-four, or more outputs.

expectedOutputExample: One lit LED runs along the bar to one end.
It turns and runs back to where it started.
The run repeats forever, with a brief pause at each end.
test.successCriteria: Eight segments take part — the chip has eight outputs, so two segments of the ten-segment bar stay dark. That's correct.

firstChecks: Check the chip's orientation by its notch, then its power — VCC and MR to 5V, GND and OE to ground.
Re-check the three control wires — DS on GPIO 12, latch on GPIO 13, clock on GPIO 14.
Check that segment's 220 Ω resistor and the bar's direction, just as on Day 6.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Send a fixed byte of your own choosing and predict which segments light, then send two lit LEDs travelling together, then fill the bar step by step — every result comes from the eight bits in the byte.
